---
title: "How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: yes
    fig_width: 5
    fig_height: 5
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{How mizerMR uses mizer's extension mechanism}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
library(mizerMR)
```

# Overview

This vignette is aimed at developers who want to understand how mizerMR is
built on top of mizer's extension machinery. It assumes you have read
`vignette("extensions", package = "mizer")`.

mizerMR uses `setComponent()` for the multiple-resource state,
`other_params()` for resource metadata, and mizer's extension-chain S3 methods
for the projection rate hooks that need to know about the multiple resources.
This means mizerMR can compose with other extension packages, such as
therMizer, instead of replacing entire mizer rate functions.

# How mizerMR currently works

## The resource state lives in a `setComponent()` component

The multiple resources are stored as a single dynamical component named `"MR"`.
The component state is an array of dimensions `(resource × size)` holding the
number density of each resource at each size on mizer's full size grid.
`setMultipleResources()` registers this component:

```r
params <- setComponent(
    params      = params,
    component   = "MR",
    initial_value = initial_resource,   # array (resource × size)
    dynamics_fun  = "mizerMR_dynamics",
    component_params = list(
        rate        = resource_rate,        # array (resource × size)
        capacity    = resource_capacity,    # array (resource × size)
        interaction = resource_interaction  # matrix (species × resource)
    )
)
```

During a call to `project()`, mizer automatically calls `mizerMR_dynamics()` at
each time step. That function iterates over resources and applies semi-chemostat
dynamics to each row of the state array, using the per-resource mortality
computed earlier in the rate pipeline.

The component state at every saved time step ends up in `sim@n_other[, "MR"]`
and is exposed to users via `NResource(sim)` and `finalNResource(sim)`.

## Two projection methods extend the defaults

The built-in mizer encounter and resource-mortality rate functions work with a
single `n_pp` vector. mizerMR adds methods for the `mizerMR` extension class so
that extension-aware projections understand the multi-resource array.

`setMultipleResources()` records `mizerMR` in `params@extensions`, registers the
resulting extension chain and coerces the params object to the appropriate
dispatch class. The value is `NA_character_` because mizer creates the S4 marker
class dynamically for the active extension chain.

```r
params@extensions <- c(mizerMR = NA_character_, params@extensions)
params <- mizer::coerceToExtensionClass(params)
```

### `projectEncounter.mizerMR`

The method first calls `NextMethod()` to get the encounter rate from the rest of
the extension chain. It then adds the contribution from each additional
resource. For each resource it temporarily exposes that resource through the
standard `n_pp` and `interaction_resource` arguments and calls `NextMethod()`
again. This lets other extension methods, for example therMizer's
temperature-scaling of search volume, also affect the multiple-resource
encounter contribution.

```r
projectEncounter.mizerMR <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, t = 0, ...) {
    encounter <- NextMethod()
    for (resource in seq_len(nrow(n_other[["MR"]]))) {
        params@species_params$interaction_resource <-
            params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction[, resource]
        n[] <- 0
        n_pp <- n_other[["MR"]][resource, ]
        encounter <- encounter + NextMethod()
    }
    encounter
}
```

The exported `mizerMREncounter()` function is retained as a compatibility
wrapper, but `setMultipleResources()` no longer installs it in
`params@rates_funcs`.

### `projectResourceMort.mizerMR`

mizer's default `ResourceMort` returns a single vector (one value per size bin)
representing mortality on `n_pp`. The mizerMR method returns a matrix
`(resource × size)` by multiplying the predation rate matrix by the
species–resource interaction:

```r
projectResourceMort.mizerMR <- function(params, n, n_pp, n_other, t,
                                        pred_rate, ...) {
    t(params@other_params[["MR"]]$interaction) %*% pred_rate
}
```

This matrix is later passed row-by-row to each per-resource dynamics function
inside `mizerMR_dynamics()`.

The exported `mizerMRResourceMort()` name is retained as a compatibility alias,
but `setMultipleResources()` leaves `params@rates_funcs$ResourceMort` pointing
at mizer's default function.

## The built-in single resource is silenced

Because all resource dynamics are now handled by the "MR" component, the
built-in mizer resource must not interfere. `setMultipleResources()` does two
things to achieve this:

```r
mizer::initialNResource(params) <- 0         # abundance set to zero
resource_dynamics(params) <- "resource_constant"  # kept at zero forever
```

## Resource parameters live in two places

Resource-level parameters (kappa, lambda, r_pp, w_min, w_max) are stored as a
data frame in `other_params(params)[["MR"]]$resource_params`. The per-size
arrays derived from those parameters (carrying capacity and replenishment rate)
live inside the component's own `component_params`. This split reflects the
two-level structure recommended by mizer: `other_params()` for model-wide
state, `component_params` for data belonging to a single component.

## Result accessors and plots are methods

Functions like `NResource()`, `finalNResource()`, `plotSpectra()`, `getDiet()`,
and `plotDiet()` need to behave differently depending on whether the model has
multiple resources or not. They are implemented as S3 generics with methods for
plain mizer objects that delegate to mizer and methods for `mizerMR` or
`mizerMRSim` objects that use the multiple-resource component.

```r
NResource <- function(sim) {
    UseMethod("NResource")
}

NResource.MizerSim <- function(sim) {
    mizer::NResource(sim)
}

NResource.mizerMRSim <- function(sim) {
    n_res <- aperm(simplify2array(NOther(sim)[, "MR"]), c(3, 1, 2))
    dimnames(n_res)[[1]] <- dimnames(NOther(sim))[[1]]
    names(dimnames(n_res))[[1]] <- names(dimnames(NOther(sim)))[[1]]
    n_res
}
```

When a user calls `NResource(sim)` and `sim` is a `mizerMRSim`, R dispatches
to `NResource.mizerMRSim`. When `sim` is a plain `MizerSim` (no multiple
resources), R dispatches to `NResource.MizerSim`, which simply calls
`mizer::NResource(sim)`.

The same pattern applies to `finalNResource`, `plotSpectra`, `getDiet`,
`plotDiet`, and `animateSpectra`.

mizer creates the `mizerMR` and `mizerMRSim` marker classes dynamically when
`setMultipleResources()` registers the extension chain. `project()` then returns
a `mizerMRSim` object automatically, so the correct methods fire without the
user having to do anything special.

## Summary of benefits

| Aspect | Method approach |
|---|---|
| Dispatch logic | R method dispatch, with no repeated component guards |
| Fallback to mizer | Plain `MizerParams` and `MizerSim` methods delegate to mizer |
| Extensibility | User-facing helpers follow standard R generic behaviour |
| Correctness risk | MR-specific code is tied to the `mizerMR` class membership |

The extension mechanism in mizer is designed with this kind of layering in
mind. Using methods for projection hooks, accessors and plots keeps mizerMR
composable with other extension packages and makes the codebase easier to
maintain.
